title: Anno Pax Romana
subtitle: A UX redesign exploring how better information visibility and console-friendly workflows can improve complex strategy interfaces.

intro.heading: Reducing friction in complex strategy workflows
intro.body:
Trade routes are one of the core management systems in Anno Pax Romana. However, on console the interface forces players to repeatedly leave the Route Editor to check information such as ship cargo or island stock levels.

This redesign explored how better information visibility and console-friendly navigation could reduce friction and help players manage logistics more efficiently.

role.heading: UX/UI Designer
role.body:
This was a self-initiated redesign where I analysed the existing interface, identified workflow issues, and proposed a new UI solution supported by wireframes and interaction prototypes.

The process included UI analysis, rapid prototyping in Figma, and small usability tests to evaluate whether the redesigned interface improved clarity and reduced workflow interruptions.

problem.heading: A fragmented workflow
problem.body:
Managing trade routes requires players to evaluate ship cargo, island stock levels, and route warnings. However, the current interface spreads this information across multiple menus.

On console this becomes especially problematic. Players must repeatedly exit the Route Editor and navigate through several menus using controller inputs, interrupting the decision-making flow.
bullets: Ship cargo cannot be viewed inside the route editor
Island stock levels are not visible when configuring routes
Warning icons lack clear explanations for route problems
Console navigation requires multiple menu transitions

image.alt: Problem areas in the Anno Pax Romana route editor interface

approach.heading: Analysing the workflow and rapidly exploring solutions
approach.body:
I began by auditing the existing route editor and mapping the player's workflow when diagnosing trade route problems.

Based on this analysis I used rapid prototyping in Figma to explore alternative interface structures. The goal was to surface critical information directly within the Route Editor without overwhelming the interface.

This rapid exploration approach was influenced by methods I studied in the course AI Prompt & Design for UX Designers at Stockholm Technical Institute.

cards.title: UI Audit
cards.body: Documented the route editor interface and identified missing information needed for decision-making.

cards.title: Workflow Mapping
cards.body: Mapped how players diagnose trade route issues and where workflow interruptions occur.

cards.title: Rapid Prototyping
cards.body: Iterated on interface layouts in Figma to test how additional information could be integrated without clutter.

outcome.heading: Route Assistant Panel (RAP)
outcome.body: The redesign introduces a collapsible side panel inside the Route Editor called the Route Assistant Panel (RAP). The panel surfaces key logistics information without forcing players to navigate away from the editor.
bullets: Ship cargo overview visible directly in the Route Editor
Island stock levels integrated into the route configuration screen
Warning and diagnostic, ability to quickly identify and address route problems
Controller shortcuts designed for fast console navigation

image.alt: Solution areas in the Anno Pax Romana route editor interface

unrealPrototyping.heading: Prototyping UI directly in Unreal Engine
unrealPrototyping.body:
Instead of only designing interfaces in static mockups, I also explored how the UI could work directly inside Unreal Engine.

Using UMG widgets and Blueprint structures, I created small interactive prototypes to test layout, information hierarchy, and interaction behaviour in the actual game environment.

This allowed me to experiment with how UI elements respond to gameplay systems and helped bridge the gap between design ideas and implementation.

images.alt: A simple UI style guide used as a reference when building widgets in Unreal.
Early UI layout prototype built in Unreal using UMG widgets.
Blueprint structure connecting UI elements and item data for quick testing inside the engine.

keyLearning.heading: Designing with real constraints in mind
keyLearning.body: Working directly in Unreal highlighted how UI decisions are shaped by technical constraints and player input.Instead of designing freely in mockups, I had to consider navigation logic, widget structure, and how UI elements connect to gameplay systems.

cards.title: Workflow first
cards.body: Understanding how players move through the interface especially with console input helped determine what information should appear first and what could stay hidden.

cards.title: Engine constraints
cards.body: Building UI in Unreal made it clear how navigation logic, widget hierarchy, and input limitations affect how interfaces should be structured.
